title: Chocolate on Silk
stainChemistry: Classification: Combination. Chocolate is a triple-threat combination stain: cocoa butter (fat/triglyceride), cocoa solids (tannin polyphenols — theobroma tannin), and milk protein (in milk chocolate). Requires sequential treatment: fat → protein → tannin. Sugar component dissolves in water but the fat and tannin are the real challenges. On Silk: protein fiber — NEVER use protease enzymes, chlorine bleach, or oxygen bleach (too alkaline). Sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) is safe for protein fibers; H₂O₂ and OxiClean are not.
whyThisWorks: Chocolate contains three stain types at once — milk solids, dark cocoa pigment, and cocoa butter oil — each needing different treatment. Silk is a delicate protein fiber so the treatment must be gentle: NSD for the protein, POG for the fat, Tannin Formula for the color, and sodium hydrosulfite for any remaining discoloration.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply NSD at neutral setting and work gently into the chocolate stain. NSD is safe for silk and breaks down the protein component without damaging it. Never use enzyme-based digestants on silk — they digest the fiber along with the stain. Dwell 5–10 minutes.

2. Apply POG to dissolve the oil and fat portion of the chocolate stain. If POG alone doesn't resolve the oily residue, switch to amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. Never use amyl acetate on acetate fabrics. Blot with clean absorbent cloth. Dwell 5–10 minutes.
3. Apply Tannin Formula to address the color component of the chocolate stain. Tamp gently. Dwell 5–10 minutes.
4. Apply sodium hydrosulfite to remove any remaining chocolate discoloration. This reducing agent is safe for silk — never use H₂O₂ or oxidizing bleach on protein fibers. Dwell 5–10 minutes.
5. Flush with NSD to remove all spotting agent residue and loosened stain material. Rinse thoroughly with cool water. Dwell 3–5 minutes.
6. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize any remaining alkaline residue and restore the material to its natural state. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Dwell 1–2 minutes.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — heat sets protein stains in silk permanently; hot water or dryer will damage silk fiber
warnings: Using enzyme stain removers containing protease on silk — digests the protein fiber
Using oxygen bleach — too alkaline for protein fibers
Using hot water — causes felting and sets protein stains
Using chlorine bleach — destroys silk fiber

steps: 1. Scrape or wipe away any solid material. Use cold water.
2. Mix dish soap into cold water. Blot gently the solution onto the stain.
4. Blot gently with a clean white cloth. Repeat if needed.
5. Rinse gently with cold water. Lay flat on a towel to air dry — no heat, no wringing.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after two treatment attempts, take it to a professional. Silk is too delicate to risk aggressive home treatments.
warnings: Never wring or twist silk — gently press out moisture between towels.
Test any product on a hidden area first — silk can react unpredictably.
Never use bleach, enzyme cleaners, or hot water on silk.

neverDo: Use enzyme stain removers containing protease on silk — digests the protein fiber
Use OxiClean or oxygen bleach — too alkaline for protein fibers
Use hot water — causes fiber damage and sets protein stains
Use chlorine bleach — destroys silk fiber
fiberSensitivities: Silk is a protein fiber — enzymes digest it. Safe agents: NSD, mild acetic acid, amyl acetate, sodium hydrosulfite. Test dyes first. Never wring.

escalation.when: Home treatment does not fully remove the stain after two gentle attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Chocolate on silk, treated with gentle NSD and cold water at home. Multi-component stain — protein, fat, and tannin components. Please use enzyme-free chemistry only.

contextQuestions: Is this milk chocolate, dark chocolate, or white chocolate?
Is the stain fresh or has it dried?
Has the item been ironed or put in a dryer since the stain occurred?
What is the specific silk type — charmeuse, chiffon, habotai, dupioni?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Milk chocolate has more protein and requires more attention to the protein step. Dark chocolate has more tannin. White chocolate has minimal tannin but more fat. Dryer or iron exposure before treatment may make full removal impossible.
